---
title: "Apple-Silicon Mojo Oracle Backend"
format: html
---

::: {.callout-note title="Source anchors"}
- Orchestrator: [`OracleRriLabelerConfig`](https://github.com/JanDuchscherer104/NBV/blob/main/aria_nbv/aria_nbv/pipelines/oracle_rri_labeler.py#L46), [`OracleRriLabeler`](https://github.com/JanDuchscherer104/NBV/blob/main/aria_nbv/aria_nbv/pipelines/oracle_rri_labeler.py#L80)
- Candidate generation: [`CandidateViewGeneratorConfig`](https://github.com/JanDuchscherer104/NBV/blob/main/aria_nbv/aria_nbv/pose_generation/candidate_generation.py#L50), [`candidate_generation_rules.py`](https://github.com/JanDuchscherer104/NBV/blob/main/aria_nbv/aria_nbv/pose_generation/candidate_generation_rules.py#L1), [`geometry.py`](https://github.com/JanDuchscherer104/NBV/blob/main/aria_nbv/aria_nbv/pose_generation/geometry.py#L1)
- Rendering: [`CandidateDepthRenderer`](https://github.com/JanDuchscherer104/NBV/blob/main/aria_nbv/aria_nbv/rendering/candidate_depth_renderer.py#L108), [`candidate_pointclouds.py`](https://github.com/JanDuchscherer104/NBV/blob/main/aria_nbv/aria_nbv/rendering/candidate_pointclouds.py#L1), [`pytorch3d_depth_renderer.py`](https://github.com/JanDuchscherer104/NBV/blob/main/aria_nbv/aria_nbv/rendering/pytorch3d_depth_renderer.py#L1)
- RRI scoring: [`OracleRRI`](https://github.com/JanDuchscherer104/NBV/blob/main/aria_nbv/aria_nbv/rri_metrics/oracle_rri.py#L34), [`metrics.py`](https://github.com/JanDuchscherer104/NBV/blob/main/aria_nbv/aria_nbv/rri_metrics/metrics.py#L1)
- Mojo donor branch surfaces: `.agents/skills/mojo-nbv-acceleration/`, `pose_generation/mojo/`, and future `rendering/mojo/`, `rri_metrics/mojo/`
:::

# Intent

This page describes the explicit Apple-Silicon Mojo oracle profile. The goal is
to accelerate the repo-owned oracle kernels while preserving the PyTorch3D/CUDA
workstation path as the default and parity baseline.

# Baseline and non-goals

- **Baseline**: `pytorch3d_cuda`, the workstation profile using PyTorch3D for
  collision, depth rendering, point-cloud backprojection, and oracle distance
  with CUDA tensors.
- **Apple opt-in**: `apple_mps_mojo`, which uses Torch MPS for regular tensor
  storage/ops where stable, keeps candidate sampling on an explicit CPU
  fallback, and uses Mojo for stages that replace PyTorch3D or custom
  CUDA-style kernels.
- **Non-goal**: broad trainer or cache rewrites.
- **Non-goal**: changing oracle RRI semantics, candidate ordering rules, or
  `PoseTW` / `CameraTW` contracts to make the custom kernels easier.
- **Non-goal**: silently selecting Mojo when PyTorch3D is missing or broken.

# Kernel inventory

The concrete kernels that must move behind backend seams are:

1. candidate collision distance in `pose_generation/geometry.py`
2. path collision in `candidate_generation_rules.py`
3. depth rendering in `rendering/pytorch3d_depth_renderer.py`
4. depth backprojection, compaction, and bounds reduction in
   `rendering/candidate_pointclouds.py`
5. point-to-mesh and mesh-to-point reductions in `rri_metrics/metrics.py`

# Staged design

## Backend seams

- `CollisionBackend` already owns pose-generation backend selection.
- Rendering will gain:
  - `DepthRendererBackend`
  - `PointCloudBackend`
- RRI scoring will gain:
  - `OracleDistanceBackend`

Each of these remains a `StrEnum` and defaults to the existing PyTorch3D path.
Normal app/offline use does not set them independently; `OracleBackendProfile`
resolves them atomically.

## Canonical contracts

- `CandidateDepths` stays canonical for rendered depth payloads.
- `CandidatePointClouds` stays canonical for backprojected candidate clouds.
- `RriResult` stays canonical for oracle output.
- PyTorch3D-specific runtime objects become optional backend state rather than
  required contract fields.

## Execution model

- Python orchestrates all stages.
- The first Mojo implementation may copy stage-local buffers between Torch and
  Mojo in order to keep interface churn small.
- The Apple path remains opt-in. If the selected profile is unavailable, the
  app and offline writer report a setup error instead of changing profiles.

## User-facing profiles

- CUDA workstation setup: `uv sync --extra dev --extra pytorch3d`
- Apple Silicon setup: `uv sync --extra dev` plus Mojo setup
- Default config: `OracleRriLabelerConfig().backend_profile == "pytorch3d_cuda"`
- Apple config: `OracleRriLabelerConfig(backend_profile="apple_mps_mojo", torch_accelerator="mps")`

# Architecture

```{mermaid}
flowchart LR
    A["EfmSnippetView"] --> B["OracleRriLabeler"]
    B --> C["CandidateViewGenerator"]
    C --> C0{"CollisionBackend"}
    C0 -->|pytorch3d / trimesh / pyembree| C1["Current rule kernels"]
    C0 -->|mojo| C2["Mojo collision kernels"]
    B --> D["CandidateDepthRenderer"]
    D --> D0{"DepthRendererBackend"}
    D0 -->|pytorch3d| D1["PyTorch3D rasterization"]
    D0 -->|mojo| D2["Mojo Metal depth kernel"]
    B --> E["CandidatePointCloudBuilder"]
    E --> E0{"PointCloudBackend"}
    E0 -->|pytorch3d| E1["Current backprojection + compaction"]
    E0 -->|mojo| E2["Mojo unprojection + compaction"]
    B --> F["OracleRRI"]
    F --> F0{"OracleDistanceBackend"}
    F0 -->|pytorch3d| F1["Current pointâ†”mesh reductions"]
    F0 -->|mojo| F2["Mojo pointâ†”mesh reductions"]
    C1 --> G["Parity tests"]
    C2 --> G
    D1 --> G
    D2 --> G
    E1 --> G
    E2 --> G
    F1 --> G
    F2 --> G
```

```{mermaid}
flowchart TD
    A["PyTorch3D default"] --> B["Docs + requirements + tests"]
    B --> C["Backend enums and config seams"]
    C --> D["Mojo collision path"]
    D --> E["Mojo depth path"]
    E --> F["Mojo point-cloud path"]
    F --> G["Mojo RRI distance path"]
    G --> H["Parity + benchmark gates"]
    H --> I{"Passes on Apple Silicon?"}
    I -->|No| J["Keep Mojo opt-in and iterate"]
    I -->|Yes| K["Consider promoting default later"]
```

# Parity policy

- Candidate validity masks and candidate ordering must match exactly.
- Depth valid masks must match exactly.
- Numeric tensors are allowed `atol=1e-4`, `rtol=1e-4`.
- Final candidate ranking by RRI must match exactly.

# Implementation order

1. docs and requirements
2. parity tests
3. backend enums and config seams
4. custom collision kernels
5. custom depth kernels
6. custom point-cloud kernels
7. custom RRI distance kernels
8. benchmark and verification
